critique.summary: The article describes a personal project where the author used AI tools to analyze 25 years of scanned receipts to track egg purchases. The process involved overcoming challenges with OCR accuracy and receipt segmentation using various AI models and custom workflows, ultimately achieving high accuracy in extracting structured data about egg purchases.
mainPoints: The author scanned and archived 11,345 receipts from 2001 to 2026, focusing on tracking egg purchases.
Multiple AI tools and OCR engines were tested, including Tesseract, PaddleOCR-VL, and Meta's SAM3 for image segmentation.
The project involved overcoming challenges such as poor OCR quality, receipt orientation, and folder mislabeling.
AI coding agents Codex and Claude were used for project planning, structured data extraction, and classification.
The final pipeline combined specialized models for segmentation, OCR, and structured extraction to achieve over 99% accuracy.
The project took 14 days with about 15 hours of active work and consumed 1.6 billion tokens, costing approximately $1,591 in token usage.
critique.truthfulness: The article appears to be a detailed personal account of a technical project involving AI and OCR technologies applied to a large dataset of receipts. The described tools, such as Tesseract, PaddleOCR, Meta's SAM3, and OpenAI's Codex and Claude, are real and commonly used in OCR and AI workflows. The challenges and solutions mentioned align with known difficulties in OCR and image processing, such as orientation detection and noisy data. The token usage and cost estimates are plausible given the scale of the project and use of large language models. However, since this is a personal project report rather than a journalistic article citing multiple independent sources, the claims are based on the author's experience and are not independently verified. There are no factual inaccuracies detected, but the narrative is anecdotal and technical rather than broadly journalistic.
considerations: The article does not discuss privacy or data security considerations related to scanning and processing personal receipts.
There is no mention of how representative the receipts are of overall egg purchasing habits or how generalizable the findings might be.
The cost and environmental impact of using large AI models for such a project are not addressed.
Potential biases or errors introduced by AI models in OCR and classification beyond the reported accuracy are not deeply analyzed.
The scalability of this approach to other items or larger datasets is not explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the article about tracking egg prices over 25 years using AI and OCR technology. Commenters debate the cost-effectiveness, technological challenges, and broader implications such as inflation, data transparency, and political factors affecting egg prices.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
10 years ago I wrote a reconciliation tool in VBA in Excel. I scan I all the (mostly thermal-printed) receipts and it matches them to credit card charges. I always envisioned incorporating OCR to automatically extract the totals, but the libraries were never good enough for my taste (and I've used industry-leading ones in work settings that process millions of reads a day).

So instead, I made a very simple UI where you just key in the amount (literally 5 keystrokes on average per image) and it finds the matching charge (or hit enter to instantly cycle through all matches). I've done bookeeping/taxes that way for a decade and keying has never been the bottleneck.

Recently I realized Amazon accounts for around a third of my credit card charges, by volume (yikes!). Unfortunately their transactions are more difficult to reconcile as portions of orders are charged piecemeal as they ship. Further, their webpage that is supposed to list your credit card charges with the matching order numbers is broken (lots of data missing - have reproduced and filed a bug report with their exec team which is still being worked on a month later).

So I wrote another tool. You download your order data and invoices via a personal data request, and it goes out and reconciles all of them. I wind up with a nice spreadsheet i can scroll around in, and whenever the cursor hits a row with an Amazon charge all the paperwork along with a generated order summary (granular down to the shipments and items) comes up on the screen to the right.

Pretty slick. And took less time to code up than his vibecoded project (but hats off to him anyway, sounds like a nice little project to hone your AI skills on). Sometimes these simple little bespoke tools are a far superior "productivity force multiplier" than fancy, generic commercial equivalents.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, practical perspective on the limitations of OCR and the value of bespoke manual tools, reflecting real-world experience and a balanced view.

comments.author: cheschire
comments.text:
Absolutely loved the article, the process, and the results. Hated the price.

You could pay a human to read receipts, 1 every 30 seconds (that’s slow!), $15/hr (twice the US federal minimum wage!), plus tax and overhead ($15x1.35) comes out to $20.25/hr over 5 hours. $101 all in.

Sure, sure, a human solution doesn’t scale. But this sort of project makes me feel like we haven’t hit the industrialization moment that i thought we had quite yet.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a reasonable cost analysis comparing human labor to automation, highlighting the economic challenges of scaling such projects.

comments.author: ProllyInfamous
comments.text:
>Everyone needs a rewarding hobby. I’ve been scanning all of my receipts since 2001. I never typed in a single price - just kept the images. I figured someday the technology to read them would catch up, and the data would be interesting.

This is perhaps among the best openers I've ever read.

[spoiler: the tech caught up, the data is interesting]

I read a lot. This article, entirely.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a patient, long-term perspective on technology development and validates the article's premise about data usefulness.

comments.author: egeozcan
comments.text:
I usually avoid shallow comments but I feel like this time it has to be said as a conversation starter: That's a lot of eggs!

Also ignoring the benefits of subscriptions, an estimate in the magnitude of thousands of dollars for extracting egg prices still makes me feel like we aren't "there" yet. This should have been a problem with a much more efficient solution given the advancements in the AI, data analysis and OCR space. I am sort of disillusioned.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a reasonable skepticism about current technological efficiency and cost, aligning with broader concerns about AI progress.

comments.author: JumpCrisscross
comments.text:
Expensive eggs are a political choice. Canada has eggs [1]. Mexico, too [2]. Meanwhile we have Tyson notching record profits [3] while facing zero antitrust scrutiny.

[1] https://www.npr.org/2025/03/18/nx-s1-5330454/egg-shortages-r...

[2] https://www.globalproductprices.com/rankings/egg_prices/

[3] https://farmaction.us/farm-action-calls-for-an-investigation...
comments.analysis: This comment introduces a broader political and economic context, supported by references, adding depth to the discussion about egg prices.

comments.author: ismailmaj
comments.text: The AI writing of the article made me give up halfway through. It’s a neat idea but the writing style of these AI models is brain-grating, especially when it’s the wrong style choice for this kind of technical report.
comments.analysis: The comment provides an informed technical critique about OCR technology trends, likely accurate given recent advances.

comments.author: jtwaleson
comments.text: Hmm, I've been sending receipts straight into Gemini 3 Flash and it handles them just fine. No need for this whole pipeline and definitely MUCH cheaper. Am I missing something?
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical alternative solution, suggesting simpler and cost-effective approaches exist.

comments.author: eeixlk
comments.text: Apart from the comical cost of extracting this data from paper receipts, is it more likely that stores will publish their product costs over time so trends can be observed or be more like gas stations where no prices are listed.  I have no idea why a box of Cheerios costs $7 for processed oats but i see millions of reasons to obscure that data.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully discusses data transparency challenges in retail pricing, a plausible barrier to easy price tracking.

comments.author: MarceliusK
comments.text: Overall this feels less like a quirky egg project and more like a blueprint for how messy real-world data pipelines are going to look going forward
comments.analysis: This comment captures the broader significance of the project as a case study in data engineering complexity.

comments.author: rdiddly
comments.text: This is the perfect job for AI, in that it's handling work the human didn't care enough to do manually. Although of course I don't care either. No value judgment there, just an observation. Imagine a place - a field let's say, part of a farm, long ago, but it had a road built through it, and thereby became a non-place, a patch of ground nobody dwells in or pays attention to or cares about, because when they're on it they're always heading somewhere else. The AI phenomenon is like that.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a poetic metaphor about AI's role in automating tedious tasks, though somewhat abstract and subjective.
